text: The surgeon identifies a dimple indicating the hernia and begins establishing the correct plane for the peritoneal flap, entering the pre-peritoneal space. The dissection progresses inferiorly, avoiding the retro-rectus space, with visible fat and a potential arcuate line. The preperitoneal space is developed to position the mesh outside the abdominal cavity, away from the bowels.

The surgeon continues to dissect superiorly, maintaining the integrity of the preperitoneal plane while preserving the posterior layer of the rectus sheath. Sharp and electrocautery dissection techniques are used to develop the space.

The surgeon identifies the hernia defect, characterized by the 'volcano sign,' as it protrudes into the preperitoneal space. The area above and below the hernia is developed, and the surgeon works inferiorly, removing fat from a small port site hernia.

The surgeon begins addressing the hernia and hernia sac, extracting preperitoneal fat from the defect. The preperitoneal hernia fat is carefully removed from the abdominal cavity while maintaining the integrity of the peritoneal flap and preserving the preperitoneal space.

The surgeon identifies a piece of fat stuck in the developed space and proceeds to close the hernia defect using a locking V-lock suture. The suture is applied by taking multiple bites of fascia above the defect, imbricating the hernia sac to close the dead space and reduce seroma formation. The suture is left slightly loose initially and sequentially tightened as the procedure progresses.

The surgeon closes the main hernia defect using a V-lock suture, taking healthy bites of fascia. The suture is sequentially tightened as the surgeon moves inferiorly to address a smaller port site hernia defect. The surgeon takes two bites on either side of the smaller defect to close it, occasionally adjusting pneumoperitoneum pressure to facilitate closure.

The surgeon completes the suturing process by backing the V-lock suture on itself to secure the closure. After taking a couple of bites of fascia, the suture is cut, finalizing the closure of the defect.

The surgeon places a piece of permanent synthetic mesh into the pre-peritoneal space, ensuring wide overlap. Vicryl interrupted sutures are used to fixate the mesh at the four corners, keeping it tented and flat to prevent wrinkling or curling.

The surgeon fixates the mesh inferiorly using a single suture for efficiency, ensuring the mesh is securely in place. The peritoneal flap is then closed with a V-lock suture and absorbable trio, maintaining the integrity of the surgical site.

The surgeon demonstrates the pre-peritoneal TAP technique, positioning the mesh outside the abdominal cavity to avoid bowel exposure. The mesh is placed with a wide overlap, highlighting the advantages of minimally invasive trans-abdominal approaches.

The surgeon utilizes a three-port technique with three eight-millimeter incisions to close the flap, ensuring the mesh is excluded from the abdominal cavity. The flap is run back on itself to lock it, completing the procedure.
The surgeon completes the closure of the preperitoneal space with the mesh positioned outside the abdominal cavity, ensuring a secure repair.
